  1. Weekly Schedule
  2. Week 2 — Design

Week 2 — Design

Prototyping, firmware setup, and character development
// WEEK TWO  ·  第二周
Design
设计

You have a concept. Now make it real — at low fidelity first, then higher. Week 2 is about rapid prototyping and fearless iteration: electronics on a breadboard, firmware running hello-world, and a 3D sketch of the body.

Learning Objectives

By the end of Week 2 you will be able to:

  • Wire a basic ESP32 circuit with a display or sensor
  • Flash firmware using the ESP-Claw framework
  • Send your first message to an LLM via OpenRouter and receive a response
  • Sketch and prototype the robot’s physical form
  • Run a first user test (lo-fi prototype, real feedback)

Sessions

Session 4 — Electronics & Firmware First Contact

Get the hardware talking.

Note

This is often the most intimidating session. Don’t worry — by the end of it your board will be blinking, displaying text, or responding to a button press. That’s real.

Agenda: - Intro to ESP32 GPIO, I2C, and SPI interfaces - Wire up a display (OLED or LCD) on a breadboard - Flash a starter ESP-Claw sketch - Make the display show your robot’s name - Troubleshooting time with instructors

Tools needed:

ESP32 dev board + USB cable
Breadboard + jumper wires
OLED display (SSD1306) or LCD
Arduino IDE or VS Code + PlatformIO

Session 5 — LLM Integration via OpenRouter

Connect your robot’s brain to an AI model.

TipWhy OpenRouter?

OpenRouter provides a single API that routes to any LLM — Qwen, DeepSeek, Claude, and others. You write one integration and can swap models with a single parameter change. No re-wiring required.

Agenda: - OpenRouter account setup + API key - HTTP request from ESP32 to OpenRouter API - Parse JSON response and display reply on screen - Prompt engineering: write a system prompt that gives your robot its personality - Compare two models (e.g. Qwen vs DeepSeek) — what’s different?

Starter prompt template:

You are [robot name], a [personality adjective] AI companion
designed for [target user].

You respond in [language preference], in [tone] — no more than
2 sentences per reply. You never break character.

When someone says hello, you respond with your signature greeting.

Session 6 — Form & Character Development

Guest lecture: JunJun Shi 史珺珺 on “Empathy as the starting point for designing robot personality.”

Agenda: - Guest lecture: how human insight shapes a robot’s soul - Workshop: translate your personality brief into physical form - Sketch 3 body shapes — which one feels like your robot? - Rapid paper prototype: cut, fold, tape — 30 minutes - First user test: show the paper prototype to someone outside your team - Iterate: one change based on feedback

ImportantLo-fi is the point

Paper prototypes aren’t “not done yet.” They’re a tool. The goal is to learn fast and cheaply before committing to 3D printing.

✓ Week 2 Deliverables

Resources for Week 2

  • ESP-Claw Getting Started — official docs
  • OpenRouter API docs — model list, pricing, API reference
  • Qwen model card — capabilities and prompting tips
  • DeepSeek documentation — Chinese-language model strengths
  • Claude API docs — for Claude-specific prompting
